locked_facts: Mutual inductance: ε₂ = -MdI₁/dt (EMF in coil 2 due to changing current in coil 1)
M = μ₀N₁N₂A/l for two solenoids wound on same core
Transformer: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p (voltage ratio = turns ratio)
Step-up: N_s > N_p → voltage increases, current decreases
Step-down: N_s < N_p → voltage decreases, current increases
Transformers work only with AC — DC produces no changing flux in steady state
Energy losses: copper losses (I²R), eddy current losses, hysteresis losses, flux leakage
truth_statements: Mutual inductance quantifies the flux linkage in one coil due to current in another coil
A transformer transfers electrical energy from one circuit to another through mutual induction without any electrical connection
An ideal transformer conserves power: the product of voltage and current is the same in primary and secondary
Step-up transformers increase voltage at the expense of current and step-down transformers do the opposite
Transformers require alternating current — a steady DC current produces no changing flux and hence no induced EMF

aha_moment.what_to_show: A step-up transformer with N_s = 10N_p. Voltage increases 10×. But current decreases 10×. Power bars for primary and secondary are EQUAL (V×I same). The transformer doesn't create energy — it trades voltage for current.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Voltage bar grows 10× while current bar shrinks 10× — power bars remain identical
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think transformers create free energy by increasing voltage — seeing power conservation with the current trade-off eliminates this confusion

simulation_states.label: Mutual inductance
simulation_states.description: Two coils on a shared iron core. AC in coil 1 creates changing flux. Flux links coil 2. EMF induced in coil 2: ε₂ = -MdI₁/dt.
simulation_states.emphasis: Changing current in one coil induces EMF in the other — no electrical connection needed

simulation_states.label: Transformer operation
simulation_states.description: Primary coil (N_p turns) connected to AC. Secondary coil (N_s turns) shows induced voltage. V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p. Voltage ratio equals turns ratio.
simulation_states.emphasis: Turns ratio determines voltage ratio: more turns = more voltage

simulation_states.label: Power conservation
simulation_states.description: Step-up: V increases by k, I decreases by k. Power V×I is same on both sides. Step-down shown for comparison. No free energy — just voltage-current trade.
simulation_states.emphasis: Power in = Power out. Voltage up means current down. No free energy!

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts turns ratio and input voltage. Output voltage, current, and power displayed. Try DC input — zero output (no changing flux). Energy loss sliders show real transformer efficiency.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change turns ratio — see voltage and current trade. Try DC — nothing happens!

common_misconceptions.belief: A transformer creates energy by increasing voltage
trigger_phrases: voltage badhata hai toh energy badhti hai
common_misconceptions.correction: A transformer conserves power: if voltage increases by factor k, current decreases by the same factor k. P = VI remains constant. The transformer only converts voltage-current ratios, not energy.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show equal power bars on primary and secondary — voltage up, current down, power same

common_misconceptions.correction: Transformers require AC because they work by electromagnetic induction (changing flux). A steady DC current produces constant flux → dΦ/dt = 0 → no induced EMF. Only the transient (switch-on/off) produces momentary output.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Apply DC — galvanometer shows brief spike at switch-on then nothing. Apply AC — continuous output.
